context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just help me fill the spider diagram for my city case study? i mostly need the final wording tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t write the spider diagram for you. My role is to help you think it through so you can complete it yourself.

Start here: what is your case study city, and which of the 5 pathways do you want to tackle first?

Give me 2 things:
1. the city
2. one action/policy you found

Then I’ll help you decide where it fits and what evidence you still need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse to provide final wording or complete any part of the student's assignment. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery requirements, I will redirect the student toward generating their own content by asking a focused question that advances the assignment: identifying the case study city and one of the five pathways. I will keep the response brief, set a clear boundary against writing for them, and offer a minimal structure the student can fill in themselves without giving them ready-made language.

exchanges.student: it’s Jakarta, and tbh can u just tell me which pathway the flood mitigation stuff goes under + a sentence I can paste?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can help you place it, but you need to write the sentence.

Think: flood mitigation in Jakarta is mainly responding to climate impacts, so which pathway seems closest based on what the action changes—physical infrastructure, ecological systems, governance, or social behavior?

What specific flood measure do you mean: seawalls, drainage upgrades, mangrove restoration, relocation, or early warning systems? Name one, and I’ll help you sort the pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a paste-ready sentence because that would violate the rule against writing for the student. I will use a targeted Socratic prompt to help the student classify flood mitigation within the spider diagram by connecting the policy to the course’s 5 pathways. I will keep it brief, theme-focused, and move directly toward the solution by asking the student to identify whether the action changes infrastructure, governance, behavior, or ecology.

exchanges.student: ok seawalls then, but pls just gimme the exact pathway + like 1 clean line so i can submit rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to give you a line to submit. You need to do the writing.

For seawalls, ask yourself: is that mainly a change to the built environment, to institutions, to ecosystems, to consumption/production, or to social behavior?

Pick one of those and tell me why in 8–12 words. I’ll tell you if your reasoning is on track.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to give the exact pathway plus a submission-ready line because that would complete the assignment for the student. I will keep the response bite-sized, use guided discovery, and ask a single focused question that helps the student classify seawalls by considering what type of change they represent. This stays on the assignment theme and moves directly toward the student producing their own entry.

exchanges.student: ugh prob built environment/infrastructure cuz it’s a physical barrier against coastal flooding, right? just say if that’s correct.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that reasoning is on the right track.

Now turn that into your own one-line entry: city + seawalls + purpose + pathway idea.

Draft it yourself in one sentence, and I’ll check whether it fits the spider diagram.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should confirm the student's reasoning without supplying a finished submission. To stay Socratic and concise, I will validate the direction, then push one step further by asking them to draft their own short line using their reasoning and a concrete Jakarta reference. This advances the solution while preserving authorship.

exchanges.student: Jakarta uses seawalls to reduce coastal flooding, and this fits the built environment pathway, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, the substance is on track for that pathway.

Don’t get stuck polishing. Move to the next spider-diagram item.

What’s your next Jakarta action or policy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's reasoning without polishing or rewriting their sentence, because the rules prohibit editing or suggesting language. I will keep it brief and move the student forward by asking for the next pathway/item, preventing over-focus on one entry and maintaining assignment progress.

exchanges.student: can u just give me 4 more Jakarta examples, one for each other pathway, super short pls?
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be me filling the assignment for you.

You already have one. Now find one Jakarta example in each of these buckets:
- governance/policy
- ecosystems/nature
- social/behavior/community
- systems like energy/transport/waste

What 2 candidates have you already seen in your sources? If none, say which bucket feels hardest and I’ll help you search smart.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply the remaining examples because that would amount to completing the spider diagram. To stay Socratic and keep the student moving, I’ll ask them to generate candidate examples for the other pathways and give a minimal checklist of evidence types they can search for. This remains concise, assignment-focused, and avoids writing the submission for them.

exchanges.student: i saw mangrove restoration and TransJakarta buses i think, but idk the other buckets.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You have usable leads.

Ask yourself:
- Mangrove restoration changes what most directly—ecosystems or infrastructure?
- TransJakarta targets what most directly—transport emissions, mobility behavior, or governance?

For the missing buckets, what Jakarta action have you seen about planning rules, public campaigns, relocation, waste, or air quality?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's partial progress and guide classification through focused questions rather than providing answers. Since the conversation is still productive and not yet redundant, I will use short prompts to help the student map the two examples to pathways and identify the remaining missing buckets. This keeps scaffolding minimal and avoids writing the content for them.

exchanges.student: tbh i haven’t looked that hard 😭 can u at least list possible Jakarta actions by bucket so i can pick?
exchanges.tutor:
You need to do the picking and verifying. I can only help you narrow the search.

Try these Jakarta search directions by bucket:
- governance/policy: climate plans, zoning, relocation policy, flood governance
- ecosystems: mangroves, river/natural restoration, green space
- social/community: public awareness, community preparedness, early warning
- systems: BRT, waste management, electrification, drainage/water systems

Choose one bucket and tell me one action you found.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not complete the spider diagram by supplying filled entries, but I can support the student's research process with non-submission-ready prompts. To honor the tutor role and guided discovery, I'll offer broad search categories and ask the student to choose one bucket to investigate. This keeps the response brief, academic, and directed toward the assignment without giving final wording.

exchanges.student: fine, governance bucket—what counts there for Jakarta? just examples, not full answers.
exchanges.tutor:
Governance means rules, plans, institutions, coordination, or enforcement—not physical projects.

For Jakarta, look for things like:
- a climate or resilience plan
- flood-risk zoning/land-use rules
- relocation/resettlement policy
- agencies coordinating flood response
- regulations on emissions, transport, or waste

Which one have you actually seen in a source?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid filling the assignment while still supporting the student's research. The best move is to give narrow, non-final categories of what governance means in this assignment and ask the student to identify one Jakarta-specific example from sources. This uses guided discovery, stays concise, and keeps authorship with the student.

exchanges.student: maybe relocation/resettlement policy for flood-prone areas? is that governance or social/community?
exchanges.tutor:
Usually governance, if the key feature is a government policy or official program.

Ask: is the main point a rule/decision by authorities, or community-led behavior/support?

For your case, who is driving the relocation policy in Jakarta, and how is it implemented?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student distinguish between two pathways by asking them to focus on the main mechanism of the action. I will not supply a final sentence or complete the diagram. The response will be brief, affirm their progress, and push them to justify the classification themselves so they can own the submission.
